elements.sent-2411-001: We describe the eigenvalues and the eigenspaces of the adjacency matrices of subgraphs of the Hamming cube induced by Hamming balls, and more generally, by a union of adjacent concentric Hamming spheres.
elements.sent-2411-002: As a corollary, we extend the range of cardinalities of subsets of the Hamming cube for which Hamming balls have essentially the largest maximal eigenvalue (among all subsets of the same size).
elements.sent-2411-003: We show that this holds even when the sets in question are large, with cardinality which is an arbitrary subconstant fraction of the whole cube.
elements.sent-2411-004: The maximal eigenvalue of the Hamming ball of a given radius is an interesting object of study.
elements.sent-2411-005: It plays an important role in the proof of the first linear programming bound for linear binary codes, due to \cite{ft .}
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, \cite{ft, BLL, sam-log-sob consider the question of whether the Hamming ball has the largest (or essentially largest) maximal eigenvalue among all the subsets of the Hamming cube with the same cardinality (at least for most values of this cardinality).}
elements.sent-2411-007: This has been partially validated in \cite{BLL, sam-log-sob .}
elements.sent-2411-008: The estimates in \cite{ft show these two values to be closely related.}
elements.sent-2411-009: One of the starting goals in this paper was to make the connection between these two notions more precise.
elements.sent-2411-010: It has been observed in \cite{llc that if $\l$ is the maximal eigenvalue of a Hamming ball of radius $r$, then $\frac{n-\l {2}$ is a root of a Krawtchouk polynomial.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Here we will show (see Corollary~\ref{cor:max eigenvalue below for a precise statement) that $\frac{n-\l {2}$ is in fact the first root of the Krawtchouk polynomial $K^{(n) _{r+1}$ (all notions will be defined below).}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: We also provide a description of the corresponding eigenfunctions.
elements.sent-2411-013: At the intuitive level, since these subgraphs are induced by a collection of spheres which are all embedded in the same Hamming cube, they should inherit some of the properties of both the Hamming cube and the Hamming sphere.
elements.sent-2411-014: In a sense, we provide a certain 'algebraic' validation of this intuition.
elements.sent-2411-015: Recall that the Hamming cube and the Hamming sphere are distance-transitive spaces, and hence support an association scheme structure, the Hamming association scheme for the cube and the Johnson association scheme for the sphere.
elements.sent-2411-016: We describe the eigenvalues of the subgraphs under consideration in terms of {\it Krawtchouk polynomials , namely the {\it $Q$-polynomials of the Hamming scheme, and (speaking somewhat imprecisely) the eigenfunctions in terms of the {\it Hahn polynomials , namely the $Q$-polynomials of the Johnson scheme.}}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Doubly transitive spaces and association schemes
elements.sent-2411-017: Both of these spaces are {\it distance transitive , namely they are acted on by a group of isometries $I$, so that for any two pairs of points $\(x_1,y_1\)$ and $\(x_2,y_2\)$ in the space with $|x_1 - y_1| = |x_2 - y_2|$, there is an isometry $\alpha \in I$ taking $x_1$ to $x_2$ and $y_1$ to $y_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In general, let $(X,d)$ be a finite distance-transitive metric space, and let $I$ be its isometry group.
elements.sent-2411-019: Each isometry $\alpha \in I$ corresponds to a linear operator $T_{\alpha}$ acting on the functions on $X$, which is defined in the following way: $\(T_{\alpha f\)(x) = f(\alpha(x))$.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The linear space of operators commuting with all the isometries $\{T_{\alpha}\}_{\alpha \in I}$ is closed under composition.
elements.sent-2411-022: This space is called the {\it Bose-Mesner algebra of $X$, or of the related association scheme.}
elements.sent-2411-023: The dimension of this space is the number $k$ of distinct distances in $X$.
elements.sent-2411-024: Let these distances be $0 = d_0, d_1,...,d_{k-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-025: The Bose-Mesner algebra has two important bases.
elements.sent-2411-026: The operators in the first basis are represented in the standard basis of the functions on $X$ by the {\it adjacency matrices $A_0,...,A_{k-1}$, where $A_j$ is an $|X| \times |X|$ matrix, given by $A_j\(x_1,x_2\) = \left\{\begin{array {cc 1 d\(x_1,x_2\) = d_j 0 \mathrm{otherwise \end{array}\right.$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The second basis is formed by the orthogonal projections $E_0,...,E_t$ on eigenspaces $V_0,V_1,...,V_t$ of the space $X$.
elements.sent-2411-028: These are the joint eigenspaces of the adjacency matrices of $X$, and we have $V_0 \oplus V_1 \cdots \oplus V_t = \R^X$.
elements.sent-2411-029: The transformation matrices between these two bases of the Bose-Mesner algebra are called the $P$ and $Q$-eigenmatrices of the scheme.
elements.sent-2411-030: A function $f$ on $X$ is called spherical around $o$ if its value at a point $x \in X$ depends only on $d(x,o)$.
elements.sent-2411-031: A function $f \not = 0$ is the $j^{th}$ zonal spherical function if it is spherical and it lies in the eigenspace $V_j$.
elements.sent-2411-033: The isometries of $\H$ are generated by the permutations in $S_n$, with $\sigma: \(x_1 \ldots x_n\) \rarrow \(x_{\sigma(1) \ldots x_{\sigma(n)}\)$ and by addition in the group $\F_2^n$, with $a: x \rarrow x + a$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The distinct distances in $\H$ are $0,1,...,n$ and hence the Bose-Mesner algebra is $(n+1)$-dimensional.
elements.sent-2411-035: To describe the eigenspaces $V_0,...,V_n$, we recall some basic notions in the Fourier analysis on the boolean cube (see \cite{O'Donnel ).}
elements.sent-2411-036: For $\gamma \in \H$, define the Walsh-Fourier character $W_{\gamma}$ on $\H$ by setting $W_{\gamma (y) = (-1)^{\sum \gamma_i y_i}$, for all $y \in \H$.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The {\it weight of the character $W_{\gamma}$ is the Hamming weight $|\gamma| = |\{j: \gamma_j \not = 0\}|$ of $\gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The characters $\{W_{\gamma}\}_{\gamma \in \H}$ form an orthonormal basis in the space of real-valued functions on $\H$, under the inner product $\<f, g\> = \frac{1 {2^n \sum_{x \in \H f(x) g(x)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: We can now describe the eigenspaces of $\H$.
elements.sent-2411-042: The zonal spherical functions on $\H$ can be described as follows.
elements.sent-2411-043: For $0 \le j \le n$, let $F_j$ be the sum of all Walsh-Fourier characters of weight $j$, that is $F_j = \sum_{|\gamma| = j W_{\gamma}$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: It is easy to see that $F_j(x)$ depends only on the Hamming weight $|x|$ of $x$, and hence it is a $j^{th}$ zonal spherical function around $0$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Viewed as a univariate function on the integer points $0,...,n$, the function $F_j$ is given by the restriction to $\{0,...,n\}$ of the univariate polynomial $K_j = \sum_{\ell=0 ^j (-1)^{\ell {x \choose {\ell {{n-x \choose {j-\ell }$ of degree $j$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The polynomial $K_j$ is the $j^{th}$ {\it Krawtchouk polynomial .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Abusing notation, we will also call $F_j$ the $j^{th}$ Krawtchouk polynomial, and write $K_j$ for $F_j$ when the context is clear.
elements.sent-2411-049: Since we will consider Hamming cubes $\{0,1\}^m$ of varying dimension, we will add a superscript denoting this dimension, if required.
elements.sent-2411-050: Thus $\{K^{(m) _j\}_{j=0 ^m$ are the $m+1$ zonal spherical functions around $0$ in $\{0,1\}^m$.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: An equivalent statement in terms of association schemes is that the Krawtchouk polynomials are the $Q$-polynomials of the Hamming association scheme (they describe the entries in the $Q$-eigenmatrix of that scheme).
elements.sent-2411-052: The isometries of $S(n,i)$ are given by the permutations in $S_n$, with $\sigma: \(x_1,...,x_n\) \rarrow \(x_{\sigma(1) ...x_{\sigma(n)}\)$.}
elements.sent-2411-053: The distinct distances in $S(n,i)$ are $0,2,...,2i$ and hence the Bose-Mesner algebra is $(i+1)$-dimensional.
elements.sent-2411-054: The eigenspaces of $S(n,i)$ can be described as follows (see Example 30.7 in \cite{LW ).}
elements.sent-2411-055: First, we define some functions which will play an important role below.
elements.sent-2411-056: We define $g_z$ as a function on $S(n,i)$ given by: $g_z(x) = \left\{\begin{array {ccc 1 \mathrm{if z \subseteq x 0 \mathrm{otherwise \end{array \right.$ \edfn For $0 \le j \le i$, let $U_j$ be the span of the functions $g_z$, $|z| \le j$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Furthermore, it can be shown that $\dim\(U_j\) = {n \choose j}$.
elements.sent-2411-059: Define $U_0 = V_0$ to be the space of constant functions on $S(n,i)$, and let $V_j = U_j \cap \(U_{j-1}\)^{\perp}$ for $1 \le j \le i$.
elements.sent-2411-060: Then $V_0,...,V_i$ are the eigenspaces of the scheme.
elements.sent-2411-062: The zonal spherical functions on $S(n,i)$ can be described as follows.
elements.sent-2411-064: Then the $j^{th}$ zonal spherical function around $o$ is given by the restriction to the integer points $0,...,i$ of the {\it Hahn polynomial $Q_j(x)$ of degree $j$.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Hahn polynomials $Q_0,...,Q_i$ are the {\it $Q$-polynomials of the Johnson association scheme.}
elements.sent-2411-066: A function $f$ on $\H$ is semi-symmetric around $y \in \H$ if its value at a point $x$ depends only on the Hamming distances $|x| = |x-0|$ and $|x-y|$.
elements.sent-2411-068: In this case, $f(x)$ depends only on $|x|$, and it is a spherical function around $0$.
elements.sent-2411-069: A function $f$ on $S(n,i)$ is semi-symmetric around $y \in \H$ if its value at a point $x$ depends only on the Hamming distance $|x-y|$.
elements.sent-2411-070: An important special case is $y \in S(n,i)$.
elements.sent-2411-071: In this case, $f$ is a spherical function around $y$.
elements.sent-2411-072: Note that $S_{y,i}$ is obtained by restricting the functions in $S_y$ to $S(n,i)$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Then, up to a multiplicative scalar, for each $y \in \H$, $|y| = j$, there is a unique non-zero semi-symmetric function $F_{y,j}$ around $y$ in $V_j$.
elements.sent-2411-075: We will refer to such functions as "zonal semi-symmetric functions".
elements.sent-2411-078: The $n$-dimensional Hamming ball of radius $r$ around $0$ is a subset of $\H$ defined as $B = B(n,r) = \cup_{i=0 ^r S(n,i)$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: That is, $B$ is a union of $r+1$ concentric Hamming spheres.
elements.sent-2411-080: Let $A = A(n,r)$ be the adjacency matrix of the subgraph of $\H$ induced by the vertices in $B$.
elements.sent-2411-081: We will describe the eigenvalues of $A$ in terms of roots of Krawtchouk polynomials.
elements.sent-2411-082: Recall that we denote by $K^{(m) _k$ the $k^{th}$ Krawtchouk polynomial on $\{0,1\}^m$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Krawtchouk polynomials $\{K^{(m) _k\}_{k=0 ^m$ form a family of orthogonal polynomials (with respect to the binomial measure $w_i = \frac{{m \choose i {2^m}$ on $0,1,...,m$) and hence (\cite{Szego ) all of their roots are real and distinct and lie in the interval $(0,m)$.}}}}}
